options: A skip list ordered for fast in-memory range scans
A Log Structured Merge tree using Memtables and immutable SSTables
An inverted index mapping terms to the rows that contain them
A balanced B-plus tree updated in place on each write
why: Cassandra uses an LSM tree instead of a B-tree, favoring write speed by treating most writes as append-only (Memtable then immutable SSTable).

options: It selects which node and partition the row is stored on
It defines the sorted order of rows within a single partition
It enforces referential integrity between related tables
It declares which columns are encrypted at rest
why: A primary key is partition key(s) plus clustering key(s). The partition key picks the partition; the clustering key sets sorted order within it (e.g. Discord ORDER BY message_id DESC).

options: QUORUM waits for all 3 replicas to acknowledge every operation
QUORUM needs a majority each, so read and write sets always overlap on a node
QUORUM routes all reads and writes through one elected leader replica
QUORUM turns off hinted handoff so no replica can fall behind
why: QUORUM = majority (n/2+1). For 3 nodes that is 2, so a write touches 2 and a read touches 2 of 3, guaranteeing at least one overlapping node that has seen the write.

options: To let messages be joined to channel and author rows efficiently
To cap partition size so busy channels avoid large-partition problems
To make recent-message reads strongly consistent across replicas
To reduce the number of SSTables produced during compaction
why: Partitioning only by channel_id let busy channels grow monotonically into large-partition problems. The 10-day bucket caps partition size and rolls over, while recent reads still hit one partition.

options: A delete writes a tombstone marker rather than removing data in place
Writes hit the commit log, then the Memtable, then flush to an immutable SSTable
Each write edits the existing row in place inside the on-disk SSTable
Compaction merges SSTables together and purges tombstoned rows
why: Deletes are tombstones; writes hit the commit log (WAL) then the Memtable, later flushed to an immutable SSTable. SSTables are immutable (no in-place updates); compaction merges SSTables and purges tombstones.

options: A central ZooKeeper ensemble holding the authoritative cluster state
A peer-to-peer gossip protocol biased toward designated seed nodes
An elected primary that broadcasts state changes to its followers
A shared system table polled by every node for membership info
why: Cassandra uses peer-to-peer gossip with a bias toward seed nodes (guaranteed hotspots) so info reaches the whole cluster and no sub-clusters form. No single coordinator.

options: 64KB per item across all attributes
256KB per item across all attributes
400KB per item across all attributes
1MB per item across all attributes
why: Each item has a primary key and can hold up to 400KB of data including all attributes.

options: A consistency mode fixed for the whole table at creation time
A per-request flag, ConsistentRead=true, on the individual read call
Automatically inferred from the size of the item being read
A single setting applied uniformly across the whole AWS region
why: Not table-level. Set ConsistentRead=true per GetItem/Query/Scan. Strong reads hit the leader, cost 2x the RCU (1 RCU per 4KB vs 0.5), and are not supported on GSIs.

options: A GSI can use a different partition key; an LSI shares the base table partition key
GSIs support only eventually consistent reads; LSIs also support strong reads
LSIs can be added or dropped at any time; GSIs must be created with the table
An LSI is co-located on the base table's partitions; a GSI lives on separate partitions
why: GSIs use a different partition key, live on separate partitions, eventual-only. LSIs share the partition key, are co-located, support strong reads, but must be defined at table creation (the reversed claim is wrong).

options: RCU cost, since only the projected attributes are read from storage
Network bandwidth only; the full item is still read and full RCU is charged
Storage cost, since unprojected attributes are never persisted to disk
Write cost, since projected reads avoid touching the leader replica
why: Unlike SQL column selection, ProjectionExpression only cuts network bandwidth. The full item is still read and you pay full RCU on its total size, which is why large items should be normalized.

options: About 3,000 RCUs and about 1,000 WCUs per partition
About 1,000 RCUs and about 3,000 WCUs per partition
About 10,000 RCUs and about 10,000 WCUs per partition
About 500 RCUs and about 500 WCUs per partition
why: About 3,000 RCUs and 1,000 WCUs per partition (~12MB/s reads, ~1MB/s writes), useful for back-of-envelope partition-count math.

options: DAX serves only eventually consistent reads, so cached data is never stale
DAX auto-invalidates only writes routed through DAX; direct DynamoDB writes stay stale until TTL
DAX caches strongly consistent reads as well, and those entries drift stale fastest
DAX is fully transparent and needs no client SDK change to start using it
why: DAX is read-through/write-through but only invalidates for writes through DAX. Direct DynamoDB writes bypass it, so entries stay stale until TTL/eviction. DAX also does not cache strong reads and needs the DAX client SDK.

options: A hash index optimized for equality lookups
A B-tree index, also auto-created on the primary key
A GIN index for multi-value and full-text columns
An LSM tree index tuned for write-heavy tables
why: Postgres defaults to B-tree indexes (auto-created on the primary key), good for exact matches, range queries, and sorting when ORDER BY columns match the index order.

options: Writes require an extension, so reads are all the engine handles natively
In most apps reads vastly outnumber writes, since users browse far more than they create
Postgres cannot maintain indexes efficiently on write-heavy tables
Read operations are metered and billed separately from writes
why: Reads vastly outnumber writes in most apps; users spend far more time browsing than creating, making read performance critical.

options: Adding an index to every column to be safe
Choosing a B-tree index for range and ORDER BY queries
Letting the primary key be backed by an index
Building multi-column indexes for common query patterns
why: Indexing every column is a trap: each index slows writes, consumes disk, and may not even be used if the planner prefers a sequential scan.

options: Creating a DM thread with its participants and first message must be atomic
Like counts can lag a few seconds and remain eventually consistent
Comments and follows need referential integrity to their parent rows
Follower counts must be identical on every replica the instant they change
why: DM-thread creation must be atomic, like counts can be eventually consistent (a few seconds lag is fine), comments/follows need referential integrity. It does not claim follower counts need instant strong consistency everywhere.

options: Noting that Postgres supports several index types like B-tree and GIN
Sweeping claims like 'NoSQL scales better than Postgres' made without nuance
Observing that reads usually outnumber writes in typical workloads
Pointing out that the right index can speed up read queries
why: Candidates get tripped up either diving too deep (MVCC, WAL) or making broad claims like 'NoSQL scales better than Postgres' without nuance. Interviewers want informed trade-offs.

options: You introduce state, since each event must remember prior counts in its window
You no longer need Kafka as the source feeding the job
You can revert to a simple stateless transform-and-write to a database
You remove the need for windowing over the event stream
why: The 5-minute window introduces state because each message depends on previous ones, unlike the trivial stateless transform-and-write case.

options: Do I actually need real-time latencies, or would batch suffice?
Can my budget cover the extra Kafka partitions this needs?
Is the incoming data structured or unstructured in shape?
Should the sink be backed by Spark or by Hadoop?
why: HI stresses asking 'do I really need real-time latencies?' Many seeming stream problems reduce to batch problems (Spark/Hadoop), saving an ops headache.

options: A crash loses all state, forcing a slow re-read of the entire Kafka topic
Scaling out demands complicated redistribution of state to new instances
Out-of-order or late-arriving events can quietly corrupt the counts
Kafka cannot deliver messages to the service fast enough to keep up
why: HI enumerates crash-losing-state, state redistribution on scaling, and late/out-of-order events as the motivation for Flink. Kafka throughput is not cited.

options: The Job Manager coordinates and schedules work; Task Managers run the actual tasks
The Job Manager only persists checkpoints, which Task Managers read on startup
Task Managers coordinate the cluster while the Job Manager just stores state
Both components run identical roles with no central coordinator at all
why: The coordinating Job Manager schedules work; Task Managers run it in task slots, which is where parallelism comes from.

options: Periodic checkpointing of operator state into a state backend
Re-reading the entire Kafka topic from the start on every failure
Disabling watermarks so that no event is ever treated as late
Keeping all counters in volatile memory and never persisting them
why: HI ties exactly-once to checkpointing state into a state backend, letting Flink recover after failure rather than replaying everything from scratch.

options: They let Flink track event-time progress and handle late, out-of-order events
They encrypt the data stream as it passes between operators
They take over coordination duties from the Job Manager
They set how many task slots each Task Manager is allocated
why: Watermarks track event-time progress so windows correctly account for late and out-of-order events.

options: Coordinating distributed systems: leader election, config, failure detection
Storing and serving large media blobs cheaply and efficiently
Acting as a high-throughput message queue to replace Kafka
Running batch analytics queries over large historical datasets
why: HI frames ZooKeeper as distributed coordination: electing leaders, keeping consistent config, and detecting failures in real time.

options: A hierarchical namespace of paths, like a filesystem tree
Data nodes (znodes) that hold small pieces of coordination state
Watches that notify clients when a znode changes
A SQL query engine for ad-hoc reads over stored state
why: ZooKeeper coordinates via a hierarchical namespace, data nodes (znodes), and watches. It has no SQL query engine.

options: Consensus, leader election, and configuration management
Image resizing, CDN routing, and payload compression
Garbage collection, JIT compilation, and thread scheduling
Sharding keys, hash rings, and bloom-filter membership
why: Learning ZooKeeper gives insight into consensus, leader election, and configuration management, the canonical coordination problems.

options: Both users connect to the same server, so delivery is in-memory and fast
ZooKeeper ships embedded inside the single server by default
The lone server still runs ZAB consensus even with one node
Messages are batched and processed offline by a background job
why: When both users are on the same server, delivery is in-memory with low latency; coordination only appears once you scale out.

options: ZAB, the ZooKeeper Atomic Broadcast protocol
Raft, the log-replication consensus protocol
Two-phase commit (2PC) across the ensemble
Gossip-based eventual propagation between nodes
why: HI's 'Consensus with ZAB' section indicates ZooKeeper relies on the ZAB protocol for its strong consistency guarantees.

options: Aged with newer alternatives, but still central to the Apache ecosystem
The newest and fastest coordination service currently available
Fully removed from every major distributed system in use today
Repositioned mainly as a general-purpose relational database
why: ZooKeeper (2008) has aged and faces alternatives, yet remains central to the Apache ecosystem in particular.

options: Client to load balancer, and load balancer to the app server
The first hop (server to client) and the second hop (source to server)
Browser to DNS resolver, and DNS resolver to the origin
Read replica to cache, and cache back out to the client
why: HI splits it into the first hop (server to client) and the second hop (source to server), each with different trade-offs.

options: HTTP cannot carry binary payloads, only text content
HTTP is request-response and closes, so a server cannot proactively push
HTTP transfers are always slower than the equivalent over UDP
HTTP forces a fresh DNS resolution on every single request
why: Standard HTTP is request-response; it works for browsing but breaks when servers need to proactively push updates.

options: IP sits at Layer 4 and guarantees ordered, reliable delivery
TCP sits at Layer 4 and is connectionless, spray-and-pray delivery
UDP sits at Layer 4 and is connectionless with no delivery or ordering guarantees
WebSockets sit at Layer 3 and handle raw packet routing
why: UDP is a connectionless Transport (L4) protocol with no delivery/ordering guarantees (spray-and-pray); TCP is connection-oriented; IP sits at L3.

options: Long Polling held open until data is ready
Server-Sent Events (SSE) streaming server to client
WebSockets providing full-duplex channels
GraphQL subscriptions tunneled over gRPC
why: HI covers Simple Polling, Long Polling, SSE, WebSockets, and WebRTC. GraphQL subscriptions over gRPC is not one of the named protocols.

options: Pushing via consistent hashing and pushing via pub/sub
Two-phase commit and gossip-based propagation
Write-ahead logging and change data capture
Leader election and quorum-based reads
why: HI details pushing via consistent hashes and via pub/sub (plus pulling via polling) as ways to route an update to the specific server holding a client's connection.

options: SSE is full-duplex, matching WebSockets in both directions
SSE is an efficient one-way server-to-client stream; WebSockets are full-duplex
SSE is peer-to-peer while WebSockets always route through a server
SSE runs over UDP whereas WebSockets require a TCP connection
why: HI titles SSE 'The Efficient One-Way Street' (server-to-client) and WebSockets 'The Full-Duplex Champion'; WebRTC is the peer-to-peer option.

options: When a database server exhausts its available disk space
When multiple processes compete for the same resource at the same time
When a network partition splits a cluster into isolated halves
When a cache and the database hold conflicting values for a key
why: Contention is multiple processes competing for the same resource at once (the last concert ticket), causing race conditions, double-bookings, and inconsistent state if unhandled.

options: The index on concert_id was missing, so the read scanned stale rows
Bob reads the same count Alice read before she writes, so both decrement
The UPDATE statement contained a syntax error that silently failed
The seat count was served from a stale CDN cache during the read
why: Alice reads one seat, then Bob reads the same count before Alice writes; both check what they read, both decrement, dropping the count to negative one.

options: Conditional Writes guarded by an expected-value check
Pessimistic Locking that holds a lock during the operation
Optimistic Concurrency Control using a version check on write
Eventual consistency reconciled with vector clocks
why: HI enumerates Conditional Writes, Pessimistic Locking, Optimistic Concurrency Control, Isolation Levels, and Distributed Locks. Vector-clock eventual consistency is not listed.

options: The thundering herd problem of simultaneous retries
The ABA problem, where a value changes and reverts between read and write
The split-brain problem of two active primaries
The dining philosophers problem of cyclic resource waits
why: HI calls out the ABA problem: a value changes from A to B and back to A between read and write, defeating a naive version check.

options: Lost updates from concurrent overwrites
Deadlocks from processes waiting on each other's locks
Cache stampedes when many keys expire at once
Clock drift between nodes skewing timestamps
why: HI has a dedicated deep dive on preventing deadlocks with pessimistic locking; holding locks across multiple resources can make processes wait on each other in a cycle.
accept: Lost updates is a real contention failure, but HI's pessimistic-locking deep dive specifically targets deadlocks; pessimistic locking actually prevents lost updates.

options: Always reach for distributed locks to stay on the safe side
It warns, in a 'When NOT to overcomplicate' section, against over-engineering low contention
It recommends making pessimistic locking the default in every service
It argues contention can only be solved by a dedicated coordination service
why: HI's 'When NOT to overcomplicate' section signals you should match the solution to the actual level of contention rather than always reaching for distributed locks/OCC.

options: Sagas, workflow systems, and durable execution
Sharding, replication, and read-through caching
Load balancing, autoscaling, and rate limiting
Indexing, partitioning, and denormalization
why: HI centers multi-step processes on sagas, workflow systems, and durable execution (Single Server Orchestration, Event Sourcing, Workflows).

options: It burns too much disk space writing verbose operation logs
It tangles system concerns like crashes and retries with business logic, growing brittle
It cannot distribute read load across multiple replicas
It demands a synchronous HTTP call for every individual step
why: Organically patching processes interweaves system-level concerns like crashes/retries with business logic, making the design complex and brittle.

options: Fanning out a new post across a social media feed
E-commerce order fulfillment: charge, reserve inventory, ship, confirm
Transcoding an uploaded video into multiple resolutions
Streaming a real-time stock ticker to many subscribers
why: HI uses e-commerce order fulfillment: charge payment, reserve inventory, create a shipping label, wait for a human pickup, send confirmation; any step can fail or time out.
